Q1: My study routine was pretty hands-on and physical. I'd spread out all my materials on my kitchen table, print things when I could afford the ink, and work through problems by writing everything out by hand. I tried to dedicate about four to five hours a day across the week, fitting it around my other responsibilities. Being a kinesthetic learner, I needed to physically engage with the material—just reading on a screen never stuck with me the same way.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot depending on what was due. I aimed for consistency, but life got in the way sometimes—bills, work, just general adult stuff that comes with being 25 and managing on limited resources. When deadlines approached, I'd ramp up significantly, but during quieter weeks I might scale back a bit to catch my breath.
Q3: Usually it was checking if there were new announcements or if grades had been posted. I'd also log in to download materials that I could then work with offline. Sometimes I'd go in just to watch the video lectures, since those were really helpful for understanding concepts I struggled with from the readings alone.
Q4: Yeah, after a few weeks I got into a rhythm where I'd check the LMS every morning with my coffee, almost like checking email. It became automatic once I realized that's where all the important updates appeared. The habit formed because I missed an announcement early on and learned my lesson about staying on top of things.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely the most valuable for me. I could pause, rewind, and really absorb the material at my own pace. Since I learn best by doing and seeing things demonstrated, watching someone work through problems step by step made concepts click in a way that just reading never did. I also appreciated the discussion boards when people asked questions I hadn't thought of.
Q6: I'll be honest, I didn't engage much with the optional readings or some of the supplementary PDFs. With limited resources, I had to prioritize what would directly help my grades. Some of the external links seemed interesting but felt like extras I couldn't afford time for when I was already stretched thin managing four online courses.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to the assignments or exams. If I could work through it with my hands—like practice problems or interactive exercises—that made it feel productive. I also valued anything that gave me immediate feedback so I knew whether I was understanding correctly.
Q8: Required activities had this weight to them that made me prioritize them even when I was tired. Optional ones felt like nice-to-haves that I'd get to 'eventually,' but eventually rarely came. There was less guilt about skipping optional stuff, which honestly was a relief given how much I was already juggling.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded almost everything and worked offline a lot. My internet isn't always reliable, and I found I focused better without the temptation to check other tabs. I'd print worksheets when I could and fill them out by hand, make flashcards, even draw diagrams on scrap paper to understand processes better.
Q10: So many times! I'd download a lecture video once and then rewatch it multiple times on my laptop without being connected. Or I'd spend hours working through practice problems on paper that I'd printed. None of that showed up anywhere in the system, but that's where a lot of my actual learning happened.
Q11: All the time. I'd access a PDF or video once, download it, and then that resource would be part of my study materials for weeks. It was more practical for me to have everything saved locally where I could access it anytime without worrying about my internet connection or data usage.
Q12: There was a period mid-semester when my anxiety spiked and I felt overwhelmed by all four courses demanding attention simultaneously. Even though my stress is generally low, the anxiety made it hard to focus. The CBT sessions I started really helped me work through that and get back on track. I also struggled when money got tight and I worried about practical things more than coursework.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from everything else going on, I'd log into the LMS but not really absorb anything. Competing responsibilities—like needing to pick up extra shifts—meant sometimes I'd have to choose between studying and paying bills. The fatigue made it hard to engage deeply even when I did have time.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd open videos or readings and just zone out completely. My eyes would scan the page but nothing registered. Usually this happened when I was tired or worried about other things. I'd technically be 'studying' but really I was just going through the motions.
Q15: I definitely postponed some assignments, especially when they felt overwhelming or when I wasn't confident about the material. The anxiety would make me avoid things even though I knew putting them off made it worse. It's something I worked on in my CBT sessions—recognizing that avoidance pattern and pushing through it.
Q16: Deadlines were huge motivators for me. I'd increase my LMS activity significantly in the days before something was due, downloading materials, rewatching lectures, checking rubrics. Without deadlines, I probably would have spread things out more evenly, but the pressure helped me focus when my motivation was otherwise low.
Q17: When I got feedback, it definitely affected my approach. Positive feedback motivated me to keep going, while constructive criticism helped me adjust my study methods. I appreciated when instructors were specific about what to improve—that gave me something concrete to work on rather than just feeling discouraged.
Q18: The course structure with its clear modules helped me break things into manageable chunks. Having four online courses meant I needed that clarity to keep everything straight. When the structure was logical and predictable, I could plan my week better and fit everything in around my other responsibilities.
Q19: The video content made engagement easier because it suited my learning style. What made it harder was when materials were only available as dense text documents with no interactive elements. Also, having to be online to access certain things was challenging given my internet situation. More downloadable content would have helped.
Q20: I think sometimes students are genuinely trying to understand the material but struggling to produce work that meets the standards. They might be researching, reading, rewatching—all that activity—but then hit a wall when it comes to actually completing assignments. It could also be anxiety or perfectionism making it hard to submit imperfect work.
Q21: That definitely resonates with me. A lot of my learning happened offline, so my LMS activity didn't reflect my actual study time. I'd download things once and work with them extensively outside the system. Someone looking at my logs might think I wasn't trying hard, but I was putting in thirty-plus hours a week—just not all of it visible online.
Q22: I think logs can show patterns like when someone's most active or which resources they access repeatedly. But they miss the quality of engagement entirely. They can't tell if someone is deeply focused or just clicking through mindlessly. They also miss all the offline work, the handwritten notes, the physical practice that kinesthetic learners like me rely on.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm actively wrestling with the material, not just passively receiving it. For me, it's about doing something with what I'm learning—writing it out, practicing problems, making connections. It's when I feel curious and invested in understanding, not just completing tasks to check boxes. It's mental presence, not just physical presence.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I grew emotionally through this course, especially with the CBT support helping me manage my anxiety. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent at my kitchen table with printed materials, or the satisfaction I felt when concepts finally clicked. It also wouldn't show how I balanced this with limited resources and still managed to pass—that took real effort that no log could measure.
